title: Certification of Network Quantum Sensing
authors: Matteo Rosati, Gabriele Bizzarri, Marco Barbieri
abstract: The distribution of quantum sensors on quantum networks is a key enabler of quantum technologies in interferometry, gravimetry, timekeeping, biological monitoring, and beyond. Yet, guaranteeing the security of these distributed sensors over noisy, insecure networks remains a formidable challenge. Previous efforts to combine quantum metrology and cryptography have encountered an apparently unavoidable tension, proposing bounds for security which are only loosely tied to the achievable measurement performance.   Here we introduce a quantum remote sensing protocol that can rigorously certify privacy and integrity of the estimation. By employing offline bilateral Pauli-twirling, our approach forces the effective quantum channel into a Bell-diagonal form, independently of the attack. Surprisingly, this also preserves metrological sensitivity without introducing additional experimental overhead. Relying solely on public communication alongside an insecure quantum link, the protocol enables legitimate users to exactly quantify their estimation error relative to an eavesdropper controlling the channels.   We experimentally demonstrate this framework by estimating an optical phase using entangled photons, observing that the users' precision consistently surpasses the eavesdropper's capabilities across a broad parameter regime. By unifying quantum cryptography and metrology, our results provide a practical pathway to achieve simultaneous quantum-limited precision and rigorous information security in real-world quantum networks.
summary: This paper presents a novel quantum remote sensing protocol to simultaneously certify high measurement precision and robust security against eavesdropping. By employing offline Pauli-twirling on entangled photon states, the authors force the channel into a predictable form while preserving metrological sensitivity. This provides a practical pathway for achieving quantum-limited performance in real-world, insecure quantum networks.
main_problem: The core challenge is simultaneously guaranteeing optimal estimation precision (metrology) and rigorous security against eavesdropping in distributed quantum sensing networks.
main_result: The authors introduce a protocol using offline bilateral Pauli-twirling that certifies both high metrological sensitivity and information security, showing the two goals are compatible.
method: They employ theoretical analysis involving bounding estimation errors (MSE) for both legitimate users and eavesdroppers, utilizing quantum Fisher Information derived from Pauli correlators.
model_or_system: The system models a tripartite interaction between Alice, Bob, and an adversary Eve over an untrusted quantum channel. The protocol is demonstrated experimentally using entangled photons to estimate an optical phase.
key_observables: Estimation error (MSE) for legitimate users vs. eavesdropper; Quantum Fisher Information (QFI); Pauli correlators ($\langle P_ eta \otimes P_ eta angle$).
important_parameters: The estimated phase $\phi$; the number of measurements ($n$); and the fidelity $F(ho_\phi, ho_{\phi+\epsilon})$.
main_assumptions: Initial analysis assumes i.i.d. attacks from Eve; the derivation relies on small-angle approximations for fidelity calculations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the concept of certifiable SPQR settings, show experimental measurements of estimated phases vs. theory, and compare total versus estimated uncertainties ($\Delta^2\phi$) against theoretical bounds.
paper_structure: The paper develops by first defining the security/metrology tension, introducing Pauli twirling as a solution, deriving mathematical bounds using Fisher information for both parties, and concluding with experimental validation using entangled photons.
why_interesting: This work directly tackles the intersection of quantum metrology and quantum cryptography, providing concrete protocols that resolve long-standing theoretical tensions in secure quantum communication channels.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Concept of our certifiable SPQR setting. Alice is interested in a target parameter ϕ only Bob has local access to. Therefore, she distributes part of an entangled state Φ to Bob over an untrusted quantum channel. At the same time, an adversarial user, Eve, manipulates the network in order to gather as much information as possible about ϕ and tamper with the probe received by Bob. We show that Alice and Bob can perform a twirling operation in order to protect against arbitrary channel attacks by Eve, but without compromising the probe’s metrological value for sensing.

Fig. 2 Photonic platform for SPQR. Photonic qubits are produced by means of a parametric down conversion (PDC) process in a 3mm-thick β-barium borate (BBO) crystal pumped by a 50mW- continuous-wave (CW) laser with central wavelength λ0 = 405nm. Polarization-encoded qubits are then coupled by means of a photonic gate, producing the state (8)., starting from the polarisation cos(π/3) |H⟩+ sin(π/3) |H⟩on both inputs. The gate comprises a partially polarising beam splitter (PPBS), which allows for polarisation-selective nonclassical interference. This is embedded within a Sagnac interferometer, which reduces the impact of deviations of the actual transmittivities from the ideal ones. Following...

Fig. 3 Estimated phases as a function of the rotation angle θ. Blue points and vertical bars corre- spond to experimental data with the associated experimental uncertainty. Theoretical prediction is here represented by the red continuous line.

Fig. 4 Estimation performances in presence of a malicious adversary. Squares correspond to total estimation uncertainties, retrieved by including in ∆2ϕ uncertainties in the CHECK and ESTIMA- TION rounds by error propagation. Circles represent estimation uncertainties ∆2ϕest retrieved by considering the ESTIMATION rounds only for error propagation. On average, Nres = 193 resources have been used in a single round. Accordingly, the red continuous line is associated with Alice’s CRB at fixed CHECK round correlators, whereas the red dashed line depicts Alice’s CRB from a multipa- rameter treatment.
